title: Freelance and companies
body: In this country, in addition to abusing the theoretically training work of interns, it is very common for companies to turn to *freelancers* to fill jobs that could be permanent. It is a way to save on bureaucracy and layoffs. The problem is that they usually demand the same or more from the self-employed than from their own employees. Exclusivity, working a certain schedule or working in certain facilities and/or with a certain team (technical and human) are usually essential requirements when legally freelancers are not obliged to do so. But a job is a job and we are free to accept it or not and negotiations are usually complicated and often leave little room for flexibility. The funny thing is that this does not usually happen when you go to other types of *freelancers*, such as the mechanic, the hairdresser or the dentist. In these cases, they are usually the ones who set the conditions (timings, rates, tools and workplace).
